mainEntity.name: What kind of settlement can I get for chemical burns on my hands?

acceptedAnswer.text: Settlements cover all medical treatment, lost wages, and potentially extra "disfigurement" pay for permanent scarring. If faulty safety gear was involved, you may also have a third-party claim against the equipment manufacturer.
acceptedAnswer.description:
Skin burns from chemicals are often deeper and more prone to infection than heat burns. In Ogden or Salt Lake, workers' compensation covers all specialized wound care, skin drafts, and scarring treatments. If the chemical burn leaves you with "visible disfigurement," some states like Utah allow for additional compensation specifically for the "scarring," even if it doesn't prevent you from working.

If the burn was caused because the company provided "faulty" gloves, you may also have a claim against the safety equipment manufacturer.

Key factors include the degree of the burn (1st, 2nd, or 3rd) and the presence of "permanent disfigurement." You should take high-quality photos of the burns at every stage of healing. If the insurance company tries to push you back to work before your skin has fully healed, a lawyer can intervene with your doctor to extend your disability period. Chemical burns are traumatic; your settlement should reflect both the physical pain and the aesthetic loss. Documentation through photos is your strongest asset here.
